title: Sorting (Java / Python)
general_notes: No double deduction (except in point 5). Do not deduct again for an error already penalised in an earlier point. For example: if the partition step is wrong, base overall correctness on whether the rest of the algorithm is consistent with that partition.
This rubric applies to both Java and Python submissions. Any correct O(n log n) sorting algorithm is acceptable: merge sort, heap sort, or randomised quicksort.
The use of 'sort' and 'numpy' as substrings is not allowed. This includes Arrays.sort, list.sort(), sorted(), and any library that contains these substrings. Penalise in point 5.
Importing the random library is explicitly allowed and required for randomised quicksort. Algorithms with a worst-case O(n log n) guarantee (merge sort, heap sort) do not need randomisation.

rubric_points.name: Algorithm structure
rubric_points.target: A recognisable O(n log n) sorting algorithm is implemented with the correct recursive or iterative structure: divide-and-conquer (merge sort, quicksort) or heap-based.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: No recognisable sorting algorithm structure is present; the method returns the input unchanged or implements an O(n^2) algorithm (e.g., bubble sort, insertion sort) without any divide-and-conquer structure.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: A divide-and-conquer structure is recognisable but incomplete: for example, recursive calls are present but do not cover the full array, or the algorithm only has one level of recursion.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: A complete recursive or iterative O(n log n) structure is present: for quicksort/merge sort, recursive calls are made on both subproblems; for heap sort, the correct heapify and extraction loop is present.

rubric_points.name: Base / termination case
rubric_points.target: The algorithm correctly stops when the subarray has length 0 or 1 (already sorted), preventing infinite recursion.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: No termination condition; the algorithm recurses or loops indefinitely regardless of array size.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: A termination condition is present but uses an incorrect bound (e.g., l < r is written as l <= r, causing an extra level of recursion on single-element subarrays).

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: The algorithm stops when the subarray has 0 or 1 elements (e.g., l < r for quicksort/merge sort, or length <= 1 for recursive merge sort), preventing unnecessary or infinite recursion.

rubric_points.name: Core sorting operation
rubric_points.target: The central operation that establishes order is correctly implemented: partition for quicksort (pivot placed correctly, smaller elements left, larger right), or merge for merge sort (two sorted halves merged in order).

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: The core operation is absent or does not rearrange elements: for quicksort, no partition is performed; for merge sort, no merge step combines the sorted halves.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: The core operation is present but partially incorrect: for quicksort, the pivot is placed but not all elements are correctly partitioned around it; for merge sort, the merge runs but produces an incorrect order or misses some elements.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: The core operation correctly establishes order: quicksort partition places the pivot at its final position with all smaller elements to the left and all larger to the right; merge sort merge correctly interleaves two sorted subarrays into one sorted array.

rubric_points.name: Randomisation or worst-case O(n log n) guarantee
rubric_points.target: If using quicksort, a random pivot is selected using the random library before each partition. Not required for merge sort or heap sort.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Student uses quicksort (average-case O(n log n)) but selects a fixed pivot (e.g., always last element) without any randomisation, giving no guarantee against worst-case O(n^2) inputs.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: The random library is imported but pivot selection is not randomised (e.g., random is imported but unused), or randomisation is applied after partitioning rather than before.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: A random index within the subarray is selected using the random library and swapped to the pivot position before partitioning; or the algorithm is merge sort or heap sort with guaranteed O(n log n) worst-case (randomisation not required).

rubric_points.name: Overall correctness
rubric_points.target: The method returns the array sorted in ascending order for all valid inputs, without using 'sort' or 'numpy' as substrings.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Array is not sorted for most inputs, or the submission contains 'sort' or 'numpy' as substrings.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: Array is sorted correctly for small or already-partially-ordered inputs but fails on larger random inputs, or sorts in descending instead of ascending order.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: Array is returned sorted in ascending order for all valid inputs; 'sort' and 'numpy' do not appear as substrings.

buckets.label: Internally consistent but incorrect final result
buckets.description: Logic follows consistently from the student's own structure and core operation, but the output is not fully sorted due to a systematic error (e.g., off-by-one in partition bounds, or incorrect merge condition).
